sentences.text: I thank you.
It's good.
[PAUSES FOR 16 SECONDS] --I guess.
I don't-- I'm tensed up.
That's all-- but that's all.
My name is Mark Turkeltaub, T-U-R-K-E-L-T-A-U-B.
Today's date is August 25, 1996.
I'm conducting an interview with Edward Appelbaum.
The interview is being conducted in Canton, Ohio.
[PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS]
OK, my own original name is Edward Appelbaum.
It used to be A-P-P-F-E-L-B-A-U-M.
We changed it to A-P-P-E-L-B-A-U-M because of the English pronunciation.
And I was born in Brody, Poland.
Not really, I had no middle name, no nickname.
[INAUDIBLE]
January 5, 1928.
I was born in a relatively small town called Brody, B-R-O-D-Y, Poland.
That's Eastern Europe about, I think, approximately a hundred kilometers from Lwow, which is the largest city in that area.
I was--
Lwow.
It used to be Lemberg, Lwow, L-W-O-W.
Yeah.
I was very young.
And the town was about 20,000 population.
And I lived there till I was about five years old or something like that.
We moved to a-- next town, which was about 10 kilometers from there, Radziwillow.
That's difficult to even pronounce.
And my dad got a job there.
And we moved there.
And we stayed there until the war started.
So we moved there approximately 1932, '33.
And we lived there for about seven or eight years.
It was mostly a mixed neighborhood.
We lived in an apartment.
See, I was very young then.
I remember sketchily only.
And we lived-- about four families lived there in the same building.
And I remember the street's name, name of [NON-ENGLISH].
It used to, to be called [NON-ENGLISH], which means happiness.
And that's very sketchy.
I don't remember-- I was only about four or five years old when we moved.
So I barely remember what--
--we moved there.
I don't even remember that.
And I-- then I was four.
I remember mostly my life when I lived Radziwillow, if I remember.
That's where I started going to school.
I remember that.
OK.
Well, it was a wonderful life.
We had a good life there.
And I went to a parochial school, which was Tarbut.
And we studied there.
It was an accredited school with other public schools.
We-- mostly, it was Hebrew and some Polish.
And it was a very good school, I remember.
I went there through six grades-- elementary school.
That's when the war started.
That was part of it, too.
It was just like-- they were teaching us Tanakh.
It was a Jewish parochial school.
But we only spoke Hebrew and some Polish.
So that was like semi-religious.
They were teaching us the Tanakh, the Chumash, and all that.
We also went to cheder there, too.
I went to private lessons with a rabbi.
Before--
--the war, I don't remember too much.
I was too young to know what anti-Semitism is.
We got along.
Where-- we lived also in a very mixed neighborhood, like our house was across the street.
There was a policeman's house, I remember, a policeman.
And I remember another Gentile lady.
Then after that was a Jewish family.
Then there was another duplex with a Jewish and a Gentile.
And we got along very fine.
There was-- as far as I know, as a kid, we didn't have any problems anywhere.
We had a full freedom there of everything, observing our holidays and going to school.
And then nobody bothered us much with anything when I lived there.
So we had a very happy childhood, a very good childhood.
We had all freedom that you can think of.
[SIGHS] In the-- what way do you mean?
I mean, can you tell me approximately--
I had a-- I don't know if you call that a hobby.
But I used to like to play chess.
My dad was a good chess player, used to play chess.
I played with other people chess.
I also took up, when I was six, seven years old, violin lessons.
And that took up a lot of my time.
I practiced an hour or two a day.
And I took lessons for two years, private lessons-- the violin.
And I was getting pretty good at it after awhile.
And relative-- they told me I was getting pretty good.
I was giving performances.
And that was really part of, like, my hobby.
I also played what they call soccer-- they call football.
They used to call it football, that soccer.
I used to enjoy sports a lot.
So that's mainly what occupied my time.
We had a lot of friends there.
We had the-- almost everybody knew everybody.
It wasn't a very big community.
So we almost knew everybody there.
And my friends from school, we used to get together on the weekends and play together.
So we did have a lot of friends.
We also have gentile friends, too, that we--
Well, that's right-- what-- I was thinking what you were thinking about.
And they were very nice to us.
And they cooperated.
I don't remember any major incidents of anti-Semitism except one.
We were playing together with some Polish and Ukrainian friends.
And I, by accident, threw a stone-- landed on this kid's head and started a big riot.
That is, he called his brother-- older brother.
He came.
He beat me up.
That's the only incident I remember since pre-war that I had.
Because I was Jewish, I think, mostly because I was Jewish.
And they were-- we know that because they just-- he wouldn't stop beating me.
People kept screaming there, telling him to stop.
He wouldn't.
He beat me over the face and all that.
He was anti-Semitic.
That particular guy lived close to our school where I went to.
And I knew-- we knew that he was anti-Jewish.
It must have been around 1936.
I was about, what, eight years old, seven, eight years old.
You're talking only Jewish people?
You're talking--
--general?
In general, they used to have stores, different-- there was a whole, full street-- a whole street of different stores, like grocery stores and also linens.
And I remember photographic equipment and other things, just a variety of things.
It was like a big shopping center-- was what we call now a shopping center.
It was a whole street of stores.
And that's what a lot of the people that I remember were in that business.
There were some professionals that worked in other fields.
There were [LAUGHS] only two, three doctors in the whole city.
One was Jewish, and two were Polish.
And I remember my doctor was a Polish guy.
And he was very good to me.
He was very nice.
When I was sick, he used to come to me and make house calls.
And he was a good doctor.
I remember him.
He was Polish, yeah.
And there was another doctor which was Jewish.
And I didn't know him that well.
But he used to take care-- he was my family doctor.
My father's-- was an accountant.
And he-- the reason we moved to Radziwillow because there were three large flour mills, very huge flour mills.
They supplied the whole region with flour.
And he was an accountant in one of those mills.
And that's where we stayed, where we lived, made a residency there before the war.
We were relatively religious, but not overly.
We were doing candles on Friday night.
We were observing the Sabbath.
We used to go to shul on Shabbat.
And we were observing the Shabbat.
They was really strict over there, a lot stricter than here, you know.
And we were observing very close.
But we were not Orthodox.
We were more-- actually everybody was Orthodox at that time--
Traditional-- we were traditional, yeah.
So we didn't ride on the Shabbat.
We didn't do any work.
And my mother worked-- there was-- the mills belonged to Jewish owners, so they always-- they had the Shabbat off.
That was my best day of my life because we were together as a family.
My dad used to come home.
And we used to take walks, used to go to a little picnic, I remember, to a little field and play chess.
And just sometimes friends went with us.
Some of their friends went with us and their kids.
We just had a ball.
It was wonderful.
She was a-- strictly a housewife.
She was a very good mother.
She was a housewife.
She used to cook and bake and keep the house clean.
I remember that-- [INAUDIBLE].
She was strictly that, no profession, nothing profession.
[SIGHS] I remember they used to go out to-- they didn't-- I don't think she had too many hobbies.
But they used to go out, like, to a movie, which was a big deal at that time.
It was just the beginnings of the movies, especially sound movies.
I remember the first sound movie I went to.
It was-- they took me to it.
And it was just something that I'll never forget because we never saw sound on the--
It was an American film.
But I don't remember the name of the film.
And after that, it became, like, more like-- they started getting more-- because we always used to have titles in the movies.
And I remember that.
And there was a novelty.
It was a great thing for us at that time-- sound movies.
It was in 1934, '35, in Radziwillow.
I remember that.
The first sound movie came in there.
Oh, they had a beautiful movie theater.
It was very beautiful, very nice.
They had a beautiful movie theater.
And they used to always show just silent movies there.
I remember Charlie Chaplin.
I remember all these things and Donald Duck and all that stuff and the cartoons.
It was very fascinating to me.
Oh, yes, I knew about United-- I saw that Metro-Goldwn-Mayer with the lion there, and all, you know.
I still remember that.
That's-- I vividly-- I was seven, eight years old.
I still remember all details.
Well, I remember-- again, it's the same family I referred to before that the brother beat me up.
He was older.
When we were kids, as we are going back home from school, we had to go through a field.
We walked, of course.
There were no cars.
They didn't have any cars in those days.
So we walked through the field.
And I remember there was a couple hooligans.
They used to stop us and try to beat us up.
That I remember.
That was in--
Jewish people coming from the parochial school home.
They want to beat us Jewish kids up.
Yeah, I remember, that was the first time.
And I came home.
I was very upset.
I said, what's going on?
And they told me to go a different route because those kids, they know about them.
And there's nothing you can do about it.
The Polish police were sympathetic with them, too.
So that's the first time-- in 1937, I remember that I felt that I was Jewish-- this anti-Semitism.
That's the first incident that I remember that happened to us.
So we used to take different routes to go home.
But generally speaking, I didn't feel much anti-Semitism there other, other than that.
In 1939-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that's a good question, I think.
I think we moved back to Brody.
My mom's and dad's family lived around there.
We moved back to Brody there.
And we had an apartment.
And in 1939-- no, wait a minute.
I'm sorry.
Let, let me retract that.
Because in '39 through '41, I get confused.
In '39-- the, the Germans started the war on September 1, 1939.
And we tried to escape.
We thought they were going to occupy us.
We rented a horse and buggy.
We took some of our belongings.
And we were escaping east.
And I remember that we were on the road for about two weeks, three weeks.
And then, of course, that the Germans stopped at the River Bug.
And the Russian occupied us.
And we came back home.
We, we went back-- that was in Radziwillow yet.
That's when we were in Radziwillow.
And then after that, we moved back to Brody there.
The family-- my mother's family, my father's family-- were there.
We moved back there because my dad got a job with them in the office, doing some distribution of food for the soldiers, I think.
So he worked for them, so they moved us there.
And they hired him to work there as an accountant.
Even-- no, that's-- the Russians did that.
That was-- the Russians occupied us from 1939 till 1941.
By the Russians--
--to do, to do the work for them, yes.
He was an accountant there, too.
And I went to-- remember, I went to school there.
I went to the general public school there, which was quite a change from where-- I was not used to it-- from where I was before.
But I remember I finished seven grades before the war started, [INAUDIBLE].
Oh yes, there, there was mixed-- it was a mixed class.
There were other Jews, yes.
I was not used to being mixed.
We were all Jewish.
We went to parochial school.
This was mixed.
But we didn't have any problems.
Everything was just going smooth this way.
The courses were taught in Polish.
And then we had some Russian we were forced to take.
But mostly it was in Polish, yes.
And then we continued with our physics and our math and all the courses we took before.
It was-- it was a little-- you know, a adjustment.
But it wasn't too bad.
I adjusted.
[LAUGHS] Right now, I barely speak English.
But I used to speak four or five different languages before.
But I have not--
That's correct, Yiddish, Polish.
I used to speak fluent Hebrew.
Ukrainian, Russian I used to speak.
And when I moved to Germany, I had learned German.
I had to learn German.
I spoke a little German, too.
OK, we were in Brody.
And then, suddenly, in 1941-- I think it was June or July-- we heard, like, explosions.
We didn't know what it was.
And that was the Germans [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] came to occupy.
That's when the war started, in 1941, with the Russians.
And within two to three weeks, they occupied us.
The Germans occupied us.
That's when the big trouble started, when they occupied us.
It started getting very bad.
First of all, they-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] hunger.
They were taking all the food from the farmers.
The farmers didn't have much food.
And there were, were rations.
They gave us rations.
And of course, the Jews suffered the most.
They gave us very little food to eat.
And there was-- that's what we felt first was hunger.
And then progressively, they did other things.
They took the-- all the intelligentsia and all the people that were educated, took them away.
And they killed them all, which we found out later.
Well, they had to register.
There was a Judenrat, which was a Jewish organization.
And they had to register there.
And they didn't know where they were going.
And they took them.
And we found out later that they killed them all.
It might-- maybe it was 400 or 500 people-- doctors, lawyers, other professionals, businessmen.
And then what was left was uneducated, which is, basically speaking, just plain-- they, they could control them better.
And I-- that's-- all these things I found out later.
And that's what happened.
That was bad.
And I think they were also sending Einsatzgruppen.
They called it Einsatzgruppen, which is the SS.
And they were just taking Jews out into the woods and killing them there.
Randomly, they were catching them on the streets.
And it was very difficult.
We had to wear our armbands with the Jewish star.
And, and we were afraid to go out.
It was-- it was pretty, pretty lousy.
In 1940-- I was born in '28.
So I was 13 years old, right, 13.
Not till after the war, no, didn't have one, no.
They didn't allow us to congregate.
We were not allowed to pray or congregate.
They were not-- we were not allowed to do that.
I remember Yom Kippur.
We went to a private house.
And we had guards posted outside.
And we were praying there on Yom Kippur in this regular house.
There were about maybe 20, 30 people in that room.
And then we were posting guards outside to see if anything happened, they would let us know.
We would-- we had to escape now to congregate.
We were not allowed to.
This was in Brody.
All that is in Brody, yeah.
And I lived there until 1942, approximate-- '41 to-- I was about a year, a year and a half with the Germans in Brody.
And then we were-- got a little lucky.
My father got hired in a little village which was about 30, 40 miles from Brody.
And he was working in a brewery as an accountant, too.
They needed somebody there that-- nobody could speak German.
And the Germans like beer.
And they allowed him to work there.
So he worked there as an interpreter and also as an accountant, chief accountant.
He knew he was Jewish, yeah.
He was known Jewish.
And I got a job in the machine shop there.
I remember working in the brewery machine shop.
So we both worked for about a year, year and a half.
And that was a lucky streak that we moved away from the ghetto, from the larger city where they started ghettos and moving people to Auschwitz.
We didn't know it at that time.
But that's what was happening.
And we were living in a little village.
There were about four or five other Jewish families living there.
And, and relative freedom-- we didn't see any Germans.
They didn't come in there because--
In the larger cities they came, mostly.
So we were, like, really relieved but still scared.
Because we were scared of the Germans.
And the Ukrainians were [INAUDIBLE].
When I say that, I mean, they were against us, and they were working with the Germans.
But they seemed to leave us alone because my dad was interpreting for them from-- and they didn't know the German language.
So they were-- they were going out of their way to be kind to us.
And maybe that was a lucky streak that we moved out there.
I don't think we would have survived otherwise.
Scared.
I was scared most of the time.
We were living on the farm.
I was doing farm work.
I was taking the cows to pasture, I remember.
I was at a-- oh, what you call it-- wood, I used to chop wood for the fireplaces.
I used to do that for us.
We lived with a gentile family, like a duplex.
We lived on one side, they lived on the other.
I used to work for them.
And they used to give us some food which was very scarce then.
But we did not really suffer real hunger once we got to the village.
We were farmers, and my dad did this extra work.
He got food for that.
So we were lucky in that respect, too.
And I remember still we were getting a bottle of beer every week for working there.
And for beer, you could have gotten almost anything.
So we were trading-- always trading that for food, bread, some eggs, stuff like that.
So we were very fortunate till about '42.
I'm talking about me, my brother and my father, and my mom.
The four of us, we stayed together all the time.
We did not separate.
I just have one brother.
I have one sister.
She was born after the war.
But just one brother, a younger one.
But then we moved to Ponikwa.
The name of the place was, was called Ponikwa.
P-O-N-I-K-W-A, Ponikwa.
And that was a lucky streak.
We moved there in 1940-- close to '42, I think is right.
That is about 20, 30 miles from Brody.
We used to order-- I'll come to you later.
We used to go on vacations in the summertime before the war.
There was big-- there were big forests with pine trees.
The-- and I had [INAUDIBLE].
I had bronchitis.
So the doctor said it's good for me to get away there.
We went there in the summer, and we stayed there for the whole summer.
And I was breathing better there, too.
The air was clear, and--
We stayed in a-- we rented a house from a farmer.
And we stayed in that house.
And they were looking for customers.
There was a few other families that came there, not only Jewish.
There were Polish and Jewish families that came there and-- for a vacation in summertime.
And we lived there.
It was the happiest-- even during, during the war-- it was the happiest time of our year-- of, of our-- of my life.
[LAUGHS] We didn't see much Germans there, too.
Next, we got the word that it's getting bad.
We didn't know how bad.
But they were getting all the Jews from the ghettos-- 1942 or so, as far as I can remember.
And they said they're going to evacuate us, take us all.
We didn't-- they didn't know where.
But all the Jews are going to have to leave.
They're going to take us, whole lot, which was, of course, I know now they went to Auschwitz and other camps.
So we were preparing.
There was about four or five Jewish families in that village, Ponikwa.
And we were building a bunker there in the woods.
They were familiar with the territory there.
So we were building with them a bunker for a few families.
And we-- when things got really bad, when they, they, they'd-- all the Jews from the villages had to move to the town, to the ghetto.
We didn't want to go to the ghetto.
So we went to the woods, in hiding.
And they-- and then the whole story begins.
Then it got rough again, completely rough.
We were--
Because we heard that they were taking all the Jews away.
We didn't know where.
We didn't want to go.
We didn't know where they were going.
They said they were evacuating.
We were just-- had a premonition that it wasn't good.
Feeling it wasn't good, you know, something wasn't right.
So-- and I guess there were some witnesses that came back from the camps and told what the-- which people didn't believe what was happening.
I didn't know much about it at that time.
We didn't have any-- no news like you have here, no newspapers, nothing.
We didn't know what was happening.
We just knew that things were getting bad.
A lot of killings.
We heard of a lot of killings, taking Jews to the woods and shooting them.
So we didn't want to go there.
From the ghettos they were taking them, yeah.
So that's why we went into hiding.
That was in 1942, '43, maybe '43.
That's why we couldn't be free.
We couldn't be-- we didn't have the freedom then to be outside.
We were not allowed to show ourselves outside.
I mean, they would shoot you on sight-- that if they saw you-- any Jewish people outside.
I saw my friend from school, from that Tarbut school, she got shot and her brother and her father.
We saw it through a--
Well, that was in 1943 or '44, I think.
We were in the hiding by a farmer.
They were hiding us.
And the next house was another farmer.
And the other family was hiding there.
And one day, they SS and Ukrainian police came.
And they took them out in the yard.
And they shot them right on the spot.
We were in another hiding place.
And I remember one thing-- that this SS came down.
We had-- our hiding place was, like, dug on the floor.
And we were going into a little stable, where they had a cow there.
And they divided that stable.
It's difficult to explain, but we were hiding there.
That SS man, after the killings that he did, he came to the-- I saw his boots in that little hall that he came.
Until today, I don't know-- I-- whether he was decent or not.
He knew we were there.
But he screamed.
He said, nobody is here!
He gave a scream to the others.
And they believed him.
Now, I don't know until today if he was just a decent human being, if he had enough killing, or what, or whether he didn't see us.
I don't believe he didn't see us.
Because we were right there under-- I saw his boots.
So I can't explain what it was.
I can't believe that he was decent.
I mean, he killed my, my friends.
But why he did it, I'll never know.
That was just a streak of luck.
And that's why we're here alive, I think, today.
Good.
OK.
